params.text:
Safe sharps, waste segregation and spillages

Sharps

Needles, lancets and blades can transmit blood-borne viruses such as hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV. To handle them safely:

  • Never re-sheath (re-cap) a used needle.
  • The person who uses a sharp disposes of it straight away.
  • Drop sharps directly into a rigid, puncture-proof sharps bin - never overfill past the marked line, and lock it when full.
  • Keep the bin off the floor and out of reach of children and confused adults.
  • If you suffer a needlestick injury: encourage bleeding, wash under running water, cover the wound, and report it immediately so you can be assessed urgently.

Waste segregation

Separate waste at the point you create it. Colours can vary locally, but in general:

  • Orange bags - infectious clinical waste that will be treated (e.g. dressings, soiled PPE).
  • Yellow bags - waste for incineration (e.g. some medicinally contaminated items).
  • Yellow-and-black "tiger" bags - offensive/hygiene waste that is non-infectious (e.g. incontinence pads from someone with no known infection).
  • Black bags - ordinary domestic (household) waste.
  • Sharps bins - sharps only, with colour-coded lids for the sharp type.

Spillages

Deal with blood and body fluid spills promptly. Put on gloves and an apron, cover the spill and use an appropriate disinfectant or a spill kit (for example a chlorine-releasing granule/solution such as NaDCC). Clear it away, clean the area, dispose of everything as infectious waste, then clean your hands.

params.question: It is safe to re-cap (re-sheath) a used needle by hand before putting it in the sharps bin.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Correct - this statement is false. You must never re-sheath a used needle; it is a common cause of needlestick injury. Drop the sharp straight into the sharps bin.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Actually this is false. Re-sheathing a needle is a leading cause of needlestick injury. Used sharps go directly into the sharps bin without re-capping.
